lede: Instead of executing tasks one by one, a program can split them into 'threads' that run concurrently. This makes things faster but introduces new problems when threads try to share the same data. Let's see how it works and how to manage the chaos with locks.

rows.dataName: An independent sequence of execution within a program.
Dealing with multiple things at once.
When multiple threads try to write to the same data at the same time, causing unexpected results.
A mechanism to ensure only one thread can access a resource at a time.

steps.title: Single-Threaded Execution
steps.desc: In a single-threaded program, tasks are executed sequentially. Task B must wait for Task A to finish. If Task A is slow, the entire program freezes.

steps.title: Introducing Multiple Threads
steps.desc: By spawning multiple threads, tasks can be distributed. Thread 1 takes Task A, Thread 2 takes Task B. They can now execute concurrently.

steps.title: The Shared Resource Problem
steps.desc: All threads in a process share the same memory. If Thread 1 and Thread 2 both try to update a shared counter at the exact same time, a 'Race Condition' occurs and the data gets corrupted.

steps.title: Solving it with a Mutex (Lock)
steps.desc: To prevent the race condition, we use a Mutex (Mutual Exclusion). Before updating the counter, a thread must acquire the lock. If another thread already has it, it must wait.
